text: HE et al.: AUTOMATICALLY IDENTIFYING CVE AFFECTED VERSIONS WITH PATCHES AND DEVELOPER LOGS 907

trustworthy, but only security patches or only developer logs are not enough to decide if a software version is affected by a CVE (incomplete).
Security Patch. Patches are a set of code changes to soft-

ware or operating systems (OS). They aim to solve security vulnerabilities in programs or products. Software vendors may release updates to ﬁx performance vulnerabilities, or to provide enhanced security features [31] . A CVE-ID may correspond to a single patch or multiple patches, and we call each vulnerable code block in a patch as a diff hunk. A diff hunk is identiﬁed by one@@statementinthepatch(seeFig. 1(b) ),anditcorresponds to a source code block waiting to be patched. For ease of explanation, we deﬁne all the lines without preﬁx in a diff hunk as an oth , and all the ‘-’ lines as sub . A diff hunk consists of four parts: the vulnerable function (including its parameters),
Fig. 2. Examples of exact and partial matching.

oth (excluding the comments), the ‘-’ line(s) and the ‘+’ line(s). Among them, the ‘-’ line and the ‘+’ line represent the lines of source code that need to be deleted and added, respectively, and oth is used to locate the ‘-’ line and the ‘+’ line. For example, the diff hunkinFig. 1(b) represents that theio_ add_buffers fromthe /fs/io _ uring.c ﬁle is a vulnerable function, and this vulnerability is patched by deleting the ‘-’ line from the vulnerable source code and adding the ‘+’ line. The patch-based vulnerability detection [19] , [20] , [32] relies on a context-based patch method, which extracts the lines from the diff hunks of the patches for identiﬁcation of vulnerable source code.
There are two types of methods for matching a diff hunk with

the source code. The exact match method means that the string consisting of ‘-’ line(s) and oth speciﬁed by the diff hunk must exactly match the source code, and the code sequence is also the same, as shown in Fig. 2(a) . We can see that the oth lines include six positioning start and end statements. The two ’+’ lines do not appear in the source code, which means this code is unpatched. Note that for a diff hunk consisting of only ‘+’ lines and oth , the exact match means the corresponding ‘+’ lines and oth exactly match the source code which has been patched, because ‘+’ lines are new code, which does not exist in the unpatched source code at the corresponding locations.
The second method is called partial match , which matches

the source code that does not have all ‘-’ lines and oth in the diff hunk, as shown in Fig. 2(b) . We can see that the oth lines include lines 4-6 and 14-18 in the patch ﬁle, which match the correspondent lines in the source code, but line 15 in the patch does not match line 10 in the source code. There is no partial match for the diff hunk consisting of only ‘+’ lines and oth ,
